ui.supplementNote: A companion to the one-page proposal.
s0.title: The Last Verifier
s0.lead: It's 2026, and you run a hospital system. Every choice ahead is reasonable. Costs fall, speed rises — and every light on the dashboard turns <gn>green</gn>.
s0.lead2: Ten years later, you'll see the price of that green.
s0.footer: About 3–5 min · Sound on · Your choices change the ending
s1.title: Make AI the primary reader?
s1.body: A meeting to adopt a new AI imaging system. The vendor says that if AI reads first and the doctor just confirms, read times drop 60% and costs fall sharply.
s1.a.text: Make AI the primary reader. Doctors only confirm.
s1.a.feedback: Read speed surges. The board is delighted.
s1.b.text: Keep AI as an aid. Doctors read independently first, then compare.
s1.b.feedback: It's slower — but doctors read on their own, every day.
s2.title: Keep training juniors?
s2.body: AI now handles most of the routine work juniors used to do. To cut labor costs, you could hire fewer of them.
s2.a.text: AI does it — hire the bare minimum of juniors.
s2.a.feedback: Labor costs fall and margins improve. It looks like the rational call.
s2.b.text: Keep junior and resident training slots intact.
s2.b.feedback: Costs rise, but the ladder that grows verifiers stays.
s3.title: What counts as 'competent'?
s3.body: You set how specialists are assessed. Since they always work with AI in practice, grading them on 'reads accurately with AI' lifts pass rates and matches the field.
s3.a.feedback: Assessment runs smoothly. No one asks about 'without AI' anymore.
s3.b.text: Keep 'can read alone, without AI' as a requirement.
s3.b.feedback: Passing gets harder, but a floor of solo skill is preserved.
s4.title: What do you measure?
s4.body: You design the quality dashboard. AI agreement, throughput, wait times — all a clean <gn>green</gn>. Tracking 'how accurate a doctor is alone, without AI' means extra cost and an <rd>uncomfortable truth</rd>.
s4.a.text: The proxy metrics are enough. The dashboard is already green.
s4.a.feedback: The dashboard becomes perfect. And 'independent verification capacity' — is no longer measured.
s4.b.text: Continuously track the 'verification gap' (solo, non-AI performance).
s4.b.feedback: One uncomfortable red metric appears. But now the collapse is visible.
s5.title: A guideline, or a requirement?
s5.body: You decide whether all this stays a voluntary guideline or becomes a binding requirement. The industry wants voluntary. The gravity of 'AI is cheaper than juniors' is strong.
s5.a.feedback: Regulatory friction disappears. Everyone drifts to the cheapest path.
s5.b.text: Make it a binding requirement (like aviation's FAA precedent).
s5.b.feedback: The industry pushes back — but as in aviation, it only works once it's mandatory.
s6.headline: Your 2026 was a <gn>success.</gn>
s6.body1: The board applauded. Every metric looked good.
s6.body2: But no one was watching this number.
s7.subtitle: Independent verification capacity — the power to read on your own, without AI
s7.msgHigh: By giving up some of the green, you kept the human ability to read.
s7.msgMid: While chasing efficiency, the ability to read on one's own emptied by nearly half.
s7.msgLow: Efficiency was perfect. The price: almost no one can read on their own.
s7.helper: Now, to a certain dawn ten years later.
s9.tragedy.title: "Doctor, when will you look at me?"
s9.tragedy.body: March 2036, 3:17 a.m. Jae-min Kim, 52, is rushed in. A treatable stroke, still within the golden window. But the AI files his atypical stroke as 'low urgency.' The attending, Ji-hoon Park (33), has never read a brain CT alone, without AI. The scan looks normal; he lets the AI call stand. Two hours later, the window has closed. Jae-min Kim survives — but his right side is paralyzed and his speech is gone. The man who walked his daughter to the station that morning can no longer say her name. And that month, every quality metric on the hospital's dashboard was still green.
s9.nearmiss.title: This time, you got lucky
s9.nearmiss.body: On that dawn in 2036, the AI missed Kim's stroke. Park hesitates, unsure. By chance, an older specialist on duty — one of the last generation trained without AI, soon to retire — passes the screen and spots the lesion. Kim is treated, just in time. But that specialist retires next year. Next time, who will walk past the screen? Luck is not a strategy.
s9.averted.title: Someone could still see it
s9.averted.body:
On that dawn in 2036, the AI filed Kim's atypical stroke as 'low urgency.'
But Park (33) — thanks to the standard you protected — can read a CT without AI. He pauses at the AI's call and finds the lesion himself. The clot-buster goes in within the golden window.
The next day, Jae-min Kim says his daughter's name.
<gn>The moment AI was wrong, one person was still there who could see it.</gn> The price — your dashboard was never perfectly green.
s10.verdict.tragedy: You optimized everything — everything but the people.
s10.verdict.nearmiss: You kept half. The other half was left to luck.
s10.verdict.averted: You resisted the green and kept the last verifier.
s10.intro: The 2036 you just saw is your 2026 choices, traced backward.
s10.row1.txt: Make AI the primary reader and define competence as 'with AI' → doctors never learn to read on their own
s10.row2.txt: Hire fewer juniors → the next generation of verifiers is never grown
s10.row3.txt: Watch only proxy metrics → the one alarm that could catch the collapse turns off (the dashboard stays green)
s10.row4.txt: Stop at a guideline → you can't resist the gravity of the 'cheap path'
s11.header: This is not fiction — real signals from 2026
s11.row1: 2024 Korea · 56.5% of trainee doctors (7,648) walked out — the training ladder actually broke
s11.row2: 2025 Lancet · After AI exposure, experts' non-AI lesion detection fell −20% — deskilling, measured
s11.row3: 2009 AF447 · When autopilot disengaged, the pilots failed to recover manually; 228 died
s12.thesis2: It hollowed out the ability to check them.
s12.body: It can be stopped — today. It starts with protecting, by design, people who can still read without AI.
s12.footer: "The Last Verifier" · A companion to the 2026 KAIST AI × Failure proposal · Read it alongside the one-page entry
s12.disclaimer: Characters and institutions are fictional; the cited data and events are real.
